flashcards.front: How is the standard matrix of a linear transformation T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ constructed?
flashcards.back: The standard matrix is formed by placing the images of the standard basis vectors of Rⁿ as columns. So, if {e₁, e₂, ..., eₙ} is the standard basis, then [T] = [ T(e₁) | T(e₂) | ... | T(eₙ) ].

flashcards.front: What is the key relationship between a linear transformation T, its matrix A, and a vector v?
flashcards.back: If A is the matrix of T with respect to bases B and C, then the coordinate vector of the output is found by matrix multiplication: [T(v)]_C = A * [v]_B. The matrix acts on the coordinate vector of v.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between the composition of two linear transformations and their matrix representations?
flashcards.back: The matrix of the composition S ∘ T is equal to the product of their individual matrices: [S ∘ T] = [S] * [T]. The order of multiplication is crucial and is from right to left.

flashcards.front: What does it mean for two matrices to be similar?
flashcards.back: Two matrices A and B are similar if there exists an invertible matrix P such that B = P⁻¹AP. Similar matrices represent the same linear operator, just with respect to different bases.

flashcards.front: What is the matrix representation of the identity transformation?
flashcards.back: Regardless of the basis chosen, the matrix representation of the identity transformation (I(v) = v) is always the identity matrix, provided the same basis is used for both the domain and codomain.
